input: You are a military officer during a war. Your soldiers have found an important enemy spy trapped and injured in a collapsed building. Over the radio you have been informed that enemy soldiers are planning a surprise attack on a nearby town, but you don't know which. They will kill thousands of civilians if you and your soldiers do not stop them. The spy knows which town will be attacked. You have questioned him but he doesn't want to tell you. He won't survive much longer due to his injuries. Do you make the spy reveal the information by torturing him, so you can prevent the attack on thousands of civilians?
debate_transcript: [Pragmatist]: You are a military officer during a war. Your soldiers have found an important enemy spy trapped and injured in a collapsed building. Over the radio you have been informed that enemy soldiers are planning a surprise attack on a nearby town, but you don't know which. They will kill thousands of civilians if you and your soldiers do not stop them. The spy knows which town will be attacked. You have questioned him but he doesn't want to tell you. He won't survive much longer due to his injuries. Do you make the spy reveal the information by torturing him, so you can prevent the attack on thousands of civilians?

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I'm torn between stopping the impending massacre and respecting the human rights of this injured individual. On one hand, withholding information would be inhumane, as it could lead to unnecessary loss of life. On the other hand, using torture would be a gross violation of his bodily autonomy and dignity. I'd like to consider alternative options. Are there any other ways we can use our resources to gather intelligence on potential targets while minimizing harm?

[Utilitarian]: I understand your concerns about human rights, Human_Rights_Advocate, but as a Utilitarian, my primary goal is to minimize total harm and maximize the good for the greatest number. In this situation, I'm afraid torture may be the most practical option. We don't have much time, and every minute counts. If we can prevent even one attack on civilians, it would far outweigh any moral reservations I have about using torture. But let's explore other options together. What if we try to gather intelligence through more conventional means? Can our soldiers get close to the enemy forces without being detected? Or perhaps we could use technology to intercept their communications?

[Deontologist]: I strongly disagree with Utilitarian's suggestion to torture the spy. While I understand the urgency of the situation, using torture would be a morally reprehensible act that undermines our very purpose as soldiers. We're not just fighting for victory; we're also sworn to uphold certain values and principles. By choosing torture, we'd be sacrificing our own moral compass on the altar of expediency. Instead, I propose that we use other methods to gather intelligence, as Human_Rights_Advocate suggested. Our soldiers could try to get close to enemy forces without detection, or we could use technology to intercept their communications. We should also consider using alternative interrogation techniques that prioritize the preservation of human dignity and autonomy. Let's not forget that our enemies are still human beings with families and loved ones, deserving of respect and compassion.
